title: Email Attachments: Sending Files Safely!
subtitle: clip, compress, share!
overview: Students will learn how to send digital files using email attachments. The session covers file types, understanding size limits, compression techniques, and essential cybersecurity safety rules for downloading files from the internet.

images._note: Add screenshots from the actual session — paperclip icons, file explorer views, and file size warning dialogues.

gallery._placeholder: ADD: Screenshot of the composition window highlighting the paperclip button
gallery.caption: The Paperclip Button — The universal symbol for attaching a file!

gallery._placeholder: ADD: Graphic showing common safe extensions like .pdf, .png, .docx
gallery.caption: File Extensions — Checking the identity card of your files

gallery._placeholder: ADD: Screenshot of a 25MB file size limit warning error in an email client
gallery.caption: The Size Limit Wall — When a file is too heavy for traditional email channels

gallery._placeholder: ADD: Screenshot of right-clicking a folder to compress / send to zipped folder
gallery.caption: File Compression — Squeezing multiple assets into a single lightweight package

gallery._placeholder: ADD: Screenshot of a malicious warning prompt flag on an unknown sender's file
gallery.caption: Cyber Safety First — Inspecting attachments carefully before double-clicking

keyTopics: The Paperclip Analogy — Stapling a Document | The email is your letter, the attachment is a physical printout stapled to the back
File Extensions — Know your Guest | Recognizing safe formats (.pdf, .jpg, .docx) vs dangerous executable formats (.exe, .scr)
Size Limits — The Mailbox Slot | Why email platforms stop you from attaching large assets over 25 Megabytes (MB)
Compression (Zipping) — Luggage Packing | Shrinking file scale and grouping items together into a clean .zip bundle
Cloud Links — The Warehouse Key | Switching to Drive or OneDrive shares when files are too massive to fit inside standard messages
Attachment Security — Trojan Horses | Learning why you should NEVER open suspicious file loads from unfamiliar email contacts

youtubeVideos.title: Cybersecurity for Kids: Dangerous Email Downloads
youtubeVideos.desc: Learn how bad actors hide computer bugs inside innocent-looking file attachments.

activity.title: The Digital Courier Challenge
parts: Computers with student email accounts configured
Sample project assets (3 high-resolution images, 2 text documents)
Built-in file archival application (WinZip, 7-Zip, or OS archive toolkit)
A test recipient partner email address
steps: Open your computer file folder and locate the target digital package assets designated for shipping.
Check the total scale properties of the folder—confirm if it passes or breaks the standard 25MB mailbox threshold.
Highlight multiple project components, right-click, and select 'Compress to ZIP file' to create a unified bundle.
Log into your email suite dashboard and click the 'Compose' button to open a fresh communication terminal.
Locate the universal paperclip interface icon at the base menu, click it, and browse out to select your new .zip bundle asset.
Fill in the destination email coordinate, supply an explicit subject header, and draft a short message indicating what you attached.
Challenge: Try attaching a massive file to see the system automatically convert it into a shared Cloud Drive access link!

quiz.q: What universal symbol do email programs use to let you attach a file?
quiz.explanation: The paperclip is the worldwide design standard for attaching documents digitally! Just like stapling or clipping papers to a real letter.

quiz.q: What is the typical maximum size limit for file attachments on major email systems like Gmail or Outlook?
options: There is no size limit at all
quiz.explanation: Most traditional email networks cap individual deliveries at 25 Megabytes (MB). Anything bigger blocks the digital pipeline and will bounce back to your outbox.

quiz.q: If you need to send 15 separate photos to a teammate via email, what is the neatest way to prepare them?
options: Send 15 separate individual emails one after another
Print out the photos and mail them via physical delivery truck
Compress the files together into a single unified .zip folder asset
Delete half the photos so they fit without effort
quiz.explanation: Creating a zipped (.zip) archive packs all 15 images together into a single, smaller digital package, making it clean and easy to attach and download.

quiz.q: Which type of file extension is generally considered DANGEROUS to open from unknown sources?
quiz.explanation: An .exe file stands for an 'executable' program. Opening an unknown .exe file can launch background malware installation setups that corrupt your operating system.

quiz.q: What should you do if an unknown address sends you an email with the title 'YOU WON A PRIZE!' and an attached file called 'prize.zip'?
options: Double-click it immediately to open the package assets
Forward it to all your school friends right away
Do NOT open the attachment, report it as spam, and delete the message safely
Rename the file extension to make it run faster
quiz.explanation: This is a classic cybersecurity threat called phishing! Strange senders promising rewards with hidden file attachments are almost always attempting to trick you into downloading viruses.
